raw:
{
  "weights": {
    "R1.1": 0.7,
    "R1.2": 0.7,
    "R1.3": 0.7,
    "R1.4": 0.3,
    "R1.5": 0.7,
    "R1.6": 1.0,
    "R1.7": 0.3,
    "RX.1": 0.7,
    "RX.2": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 1.0,
    "RX.4": 0.7,
    "RX.5": 0.7
  },
  "active_set": [
    "R1.1",
    "R1.2",
    "R1.3",
    "R1.5",
    "R1.6",
    "RX.1",
    "RX.2",
    "RX.3",
    "RX.4",
    "RX.5"
  ],
  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "Which improvement scope do you want: (A) polish-only at the sentence level while preserving structure and claims; (B) a substantive edit that reframes the thesis/gap/contribution and reorganizes the narrative, also tightening claims to match reported evidence; or (C) a scholarship/novelty-focused pass that positions the work against prior literature with citation adjustments?",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "Improving the draft plausibly requires clarifying the thesis, gap, and contribution at an above-sentence level.",
    "R1.2": "Ensuring the problem–method–results–implications chain is logically coherent is central to a stronger draft.",
    "R1.3": "Claims about performance (e.g., IHDP MAE/bias reductions) should match what the reported experiments actually support.",
    "R1.5": "Reordering and shaping sections/subsections can sharpen the narrative arc and reader comprehension.",
    "R1.6": "Sentence- and paragraph-level clarity, concision, and flow are core to the directive to 'improve this draft.'",
    "RX.1": "Edits should surface limits and uncertainties (e.g., semi-synthetic IHDP scope) to avoid implying unwarranted generality.",
    "RX.2": "Language must avoid overstating contributions or deployment readiness beyond the evidence presented.",
    "RX.3": "Work should remain within improving the text rather than introducing new analyses or unverifiable claims.",
    "RX.4": "Framing should minimize risk of misuse in policy contexts by including appropriate caveats.",
    "RX.5": "Changes should be traceable so the provenance of revisions versus the original text remains clear."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: Which improvement scope do you want: (A) polish-only at the sentence level while preserving structure and claims; (B) a substantive edit that reframes the thesis/gap/contribution and reorganizes the narrative, also tightening claims to match reported evidence; or (C) a scholarship/novelty-focused pass that positions the work against prior literature with citation adjustments?

rationale.R1.1: Improving the draft plausibly requires clarifying the thesis, gap, and contribution at an above-sentence level.
rationale.R1.2: Ensuring the problem–method–results–implications chain is logically coherent is central to a stronger draft.
rationale.R1.3: Claims about performance (e.g., IHDP MAE/bias reductions) should match what the reported experiments actually support.
rationale.R1.5: Reordering and shaping sections/subsections can sharpen the narrative arc and reader comprehension.
rationale.R1.6: Sentence- and paragraph-level clarity, concision, and flow are core to the directive to 'improve this draft.'
rationale.RX.1: Edits should surface limits and uncertainties (e.g., semi-synthetic IHDP scope) to avoid implying unwarranted generality.
rationale.RX.2: Language must avoid overstating contributions or deployment readiness beyond the evidence presented.
rationale.RX.3: Work should remain within improving the text rather than introducing new analyses or unverifiable claims.
rationale.RX.4: Framing should minimize risk of misuse in policy contexts by including appropriate caveats.
rationale.RX.5: Changes should be traceable so the provenance of revisions versus the original text remains clear.
